title: To direct the Secretary of Interior to submit to Congress a report on the National Park Service's interpretation and application of the Standards for Rehabilitation for use of the Federal Historic Preservation Tax Incentives program.
plain_summary: This bill tells the Secretary of the Interior to write a report about how the National Park Service runs a tax program that helps people fix up old historic buildings. The report needs to include information from the past ten years about how long it takes to process applications and what happens to them. It also needs to suggest ways to make the program work better for creating affordable housing while still protecting historic buildings. The goal is to help Congress understand how well this tax program is working and how it might be improved.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1  Report
sections.text: The Secretary of the Interior must write a report about how the National Park Service uses and interprets the Standards for Rehabilitation. These standards are rules that guide a federal tax program that helps people restore historic buildings. The Secretary must send this report to two committees in Congress within one year of this law being passed. The report must go to the Committee on Natural Resources in the House of Representatives and the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources in the Senate. The report must include specific information about how the tax incentive program has worked over the past ten years. This includes data on how long applications take to process from start to finish, broken down by the size and type of project and the three different parts of the application process. The report must also show the average number of applications that get put on hold each year, how many get approved with conditions, how many get denied, how many get approved, and how many people withdraw their applications. The report must describe ways to improve the standards to make it easier to create affordable housing while still protecting historic buildings. It should identify opportunities for the Secretary to update guidance to help people convert non-residential buildings into homes, fix environmental and safety problems, add energy-efficient features, make buildings accessible to people with disabilities, and remove other barriers that program users face. The report must explain what specific steps the National Park Service has taken in the past ten years to improve how they interpret the standards to support affordable housing projects. It should describe the most common problems that people face when trying to develop affordable housing using this program and what the Service has done to fix those problems. This includes details about how the Service collects and uses feedback from people who use the program. The report must explain how the Service decides when to update its guidance documents, including guidelines and bulletins that help people understand the standards, and whether lack of resources is preventing the Service from updating this guidance. Finally, the report must include the Secretary's recommendations for how to update and improve both the standards themselves and how the Service implements them. These recommendations should focus on making the program work better with affordable housing needs, protecting historic properties from climate change risks, and considering whether proposed changes are economically and technically possible to do.
